speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your hemoglobin level today is 142 g/L, which falls within the normal range for adults.

speaker: doctor
text: I also see your body temperature is elevated at 38.2 °C — have you been feeling feverish or unwell this morning?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had chills since yesterday evening and a mild headache. No cough or sore throat, though.

speaker: doctor
text: Any recent exposure to sick contacts or changes in activity? Also, how’s your energy level been?

speaker: patient
text: My energy’s been low, and I skipped my usual walk two days ago because I felt so tired.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s check your vital signs more thoroughly. I’ll measure your blood pressure next.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 136 mmHg — well-controlled for now, especially given your diagnosis of essential hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Atorvastatin 20mg once daily at night for cholesterol management. Are you tolerating it well? Any muscle aches or digestive issues?

speaker: patient
text: No side effects — I’ve been taking it consistently with dinner every night.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your persistent fatigue, low-grade fever, and stable but borderline BP, we’ll need to consider whether there’s an underlying inflammatory or metabolic component. Your primary diagnosis remains essential hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: I did notice my ankles were slightly swollen last night — not sure if that’s related.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s worth noting. Let’s reassess your hemoglobin — sometimes inflammation can suppress it, but let’s see what today’s value shows.

speaker: doctor
text: Hmm — your repeat hemoglobin is 164 g/L, which is higher than the 142 we saw earlier this morning. That’s unusual — could reflect hemoconcentration or lab variability. We’ll monitor closely.

speaker: patient
text: I drank less water yesterday — maybe that’s why it went up?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. Dehydration can elevate hemoglobin. Let’s also recheck your blood pressure after you’ve rested for a few minutes.

speaker: patient
text: Sure — I’ll sit quietly for a minute.

speaker: doctor
text: Now your systolic pressure is 158 mmHg — higher than the 136 we recorded earlier. That rise may reflect pain, anxiety, or the febrile state. Let’s address the fever first.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature has come down to 37.0 °C — much better. That likely explains part of the BP fluctuation and improved comfort.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I feel warmer but less shaky now. My blood pressure was 136 earlier — that number feels more typical for me when I’m resting.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your new symptoms, rising glucose concerns, and stable renal function, I am going to start you on Metformin 1000mg twice daily — and yes, that 142 g/L hemoglobin from your first draw remains our baseline reference point.
